# Own Your Code — Stay Here (v0)

*Generated by `own-your-code`. Disposable — regenerate after any change; never hand-edit.
This is the **engineering wiki** for the build — written so a new maintainer could take the repo
over and run it without reading the code first. Not the user-facing `README`. Read top-down: what
it is → the component map → each component with its decisions nested inside → drift → gaps → the
active pass.*

*Certainty tags: **`[code]`** verified in source · **`[doc]`** rationale confirmed in
`docs/decision_log.md` · **`[likely-why]`** reconstructed, verify · **`[reconsider]`** judgment.
Ownership lens on every key decision: **maintainability / UX / cost** (a decision is "key" when a
wrong call there costs one of those three).*

---

## 1 · Orientation — the cockpit (read this screen; drill down only where you don't already own)

**Domain primer** — the acronyms up front, so the rest reads cleanly:
- **DOL** — U.S. Department of Labor.
- **OFLC** — Office of Foreign Labor Certification; the DOL office that publishes the disclosure data.
- **LCA** — *Labor Condition Application*: the filing an employer submits to sponsor an H-1B worker at a stated wage. A **certified** LCA = the employer is cleared and intends to sponsor. **This is the core signal.**
- **PERM** — the *green-card*-stage labor certification; a different, later step. Wrong layer here; the code guards against loading a PERM file by mistake.
- **PW_WAGE_LEVEL** — Prevailing Wage Level (I–IV). **Level I = entry wage**, the new-grad signal the whole thing filters on.
- **SOC code** — Standard Occupational Classification; the federal job-category code. Design codes used: `15-1255` (Web & Digital Interface = UI/UX), `27-1024` (Graphic), `27-1021` (Commercial & Industrial).
- **H-1B** — the temporary skilled-worker visa the LCA underlies (the sponsorship-intent signal).
- **OPT / STEM-OPT** — Optional Practical Training: post-graduation work authorization for international students (STEM extends it). Design is likely *not* STEM-eligible → ~12-month runway.
- **CASE_STATUS = Certified** — the filing DOL approved; the only rows the engine keeps.
- **iGavel** — the FY2025-Q4 top single-quarter design sponsor, hardcoded as the verification "golden anchor" (see D3).

**What it is.** A grounded **visa-sponsorship diagnostic** for **international new-grad designers who need US work sponsorship** (validated so far on one real designer). It reads mandated U.S. Department of Labor filings (the **LCA** disclosure data) to find companies that *actually* sponsor designers at the **entry wage** (`PW_WAGE_LEVEL = I`, under three design SOC codes), ranked by how *repeatedly* they sponsor across quarters. A separate, human-reviewed AI step then compares a user's portfolio against those companies' real job postings and proposes three portfolio projects to close the gaps. The edge is **grounding + advice**: the shortlist comes straight off government filings, and the advice points at real, named companies — not "a directory of who sponsors" (h1bgrader/MyVisaJobs already do that).

**Current state.** v0 — built by hand, no app yet. The committed full-year run (`output/sponsors_levelI.csv`) lists **480 distinct entry-wage design sponsors across FY2025, 9 of them appearing in 2+ quarters** (Amazon top: 3 quarters / 7 filings; Deloitte, Esri, Activision, TikTok, Microsoft all present) — so the go/no-go feasibility check (the ledger's "kill-test") passed: the filter returns real, varied employers, not staffing agencies. Still pending: the **portfolio review** — the human-reviewed AI step (`prompts/gap_read.md`) that produces the 3 named-company project ideas (its output `output/private/gap_read_filled.md` isn't created yet, so the report shows a placeholder). Raw DOL files are gitignored (not in this clone). Next: run the portfolio review, then hand v0 to a tester; the one known open code task is gating the verification "golden anchor" to a fixed quarter (D3).

**Component map — two tracks that meet in one report.** *(Tags `[A]`–`[E]` anchor to §2/§3; read top to bottom.)*

```
TRACK 1 · grounding — deterministic, NO AI — "which companies actually sponsor entry-wage designers?"
   raw DOL LCA filings (xlsx)
   → [C] convert_quarters — cache each quarter as parquet
   → [A] engine — keep only CERTIFIED, entry-wage (Level I), design-role filings;
                  group by employer; rank by how many quarters each one sponsored
   → [B] verify — raise-and-stop gate (a bad result never ships)
   → output/sponsors_levelI.csv   ·   the ranked sponsor shortlist

TRACK 2 · advice — human-reviewed AI — "what should someone build to be worth a visa to these companies?"
   the user's portfolio (text + links)  +  a few real entry-level postings from shortlist companies
   → [E] portfolio review (prompts/gap_read.md) — run in your own Claude/ChatGPT; review the output before sharing it
        · names the gaps between the portfolio and those companies' actual postings   ← what it evaluates
        · proposes 3 projects, each aimed at named companies, scoped 2–4 weeks
   → output/private/gap_read_filled.md

THEY MEET
   shortlist  +  portfolio review  → [D] build_report → output/private/report.html  (private)
```

Maps to the orientation 1:1 — Track 1 = "find companies that actually sponsor," Track 2 = "compare a
portfolio against those companies' postings and propose 3 named-company projects," and the
`← what it evaluates` line is that step's actual criterion. `[A]`/`[B]` = the deterministic engine +
its gate (no AI); `[C]`/`[D]` = thin caller scripts; `[E]` = the human-reviewed AI step, outside the pipeline.

**Decisions index** (full treatment lives under each component in §3).

| # | Decision | Component | Lens | Tier | Your call | Cert |
|---|----------|-----------|------|------|-----------|------|
| D1 | xlsx→parquet: streaming openpyxl, **by-name** 12-col select | A engine | maint · cost | full | ▢ Open | `[code]` |
| D2 | Employer normalization (rule-based, conservative **under**-merge) | A engine | UX · maint | full | ▢ Open | `[code]` |
| D5 | Full-year roll-up + **repeat-sponsor** ranking | A engine | UX | confirm | ☑ OWNED | `[doc]` |
| D6 | Level-I spelling tolerance + wage annualization | A engine | maint | confirm | ☑ OWNED | `[code]` |
| D3 | Verification gate = invariants + one golden anchor | B verify | maint | full | ⚠ **REVISIT** | `[code]` |
| D4 | Engine/altitude split (pure engine, thin scripts) | C runners | maint | confirm | ☑ OWNED | `[doc]` |
| D7 | Self-contained HTML report (no JS/pandoc) | D report | UX · cost | confirm | ☑ OWNED | `[doc]` |
| D8 | Privacy split (private report vs public engine/CSV) | D report | cost-risk · UX | confirm | ☑ OWNED | `[doc]` |
| D9 | AI confined to a human-reviewed portfolio review; data step deterministic | E review | UX-trust | confirm | ☑ OWNED | `[doc]` |

**Where to spend attention (top gaps, ranked).**
1. **D1 xlsx-IO + the "streaming" memory reality** — most AI-defaulted call, the engine's
   foundation, and `engine/sponsors.py:159` materializes *every* row in memory before the
   DataFrame (streams the parse, not memory). Biggest re-derivation gap; matters most for the v2
   automation goal. `[decision + runtime]`
2. **D3 golden-anchor residual coupling** — `verify_golden_killtest` defaults to `IGAVEL` (an
   FY2025-Q4 employer) and `build_shortlist` runs it against *whatever* `--quarters` you pass, so
   a quarters-subset run can raise on correct data. Small, well-understood, the last brittle edge.
3. **D2 entity-resolution landscape** — the conservative under-merge is defensible but its
   alternatives (fuzzy / record-linkage / EIN / alias-map) are unplaced on the axes.

---

<details>
<summary><b>2 · The system — components + how they connect</b> (assembled whole; mechanism only, the "why" is in §3)</summary>

**Component roles.**

| Component | File(s) | Kind | Owns |
|-----------|---------|------|------|
| **A · Engine** | `engine/sponsors.py` | library (no `__main__`, no report I/O) | `ROLE_SOC`, `REQUIRED_COLUMNS`, `xlsx_to_parquet`, `load_certified_rows`, `build_sponsor_table`, `normalize_employer/soc`, wage annualization [D1,D2,D5,D6] |
| **B · Verify gate** | `engine/verify.py` | library — the "verification cell" | 6 asserts; raises `VerificationError` and stops the run [D3] |
| **C · Runners** | `scripts/convert_quarters.py`, `scripts/build_shortlist.py` | thin entrypoints | convert raw→parquet (1st); provenance + engine + gate + write CSVs (2nd) [D4] |
| **D · Report** | `scripts/build_report.py` | thin entrypoint | render private self-contained HTML from the CSV (+ portfolio review if present) [D7,D8] |
| **E · Portfolio review** | `prompts/gap_read.md` | **human step** (external LLM) | the reviewed advice half: gaps + 3 named-company projects [D9] |
| *(anchor)* | `docs/decision_log.md` | Judgment Ledger | the forks + why; the drift source-of-truth (consumed, not run) |

**Control + data flow.** `engine` holds all logic; scripts are thin callers.
`convert_quarters` writes a parquet per quarter **only if it doesn't already exist**
(`scripts/convert_quarters.py:39`; `--force` to redo). `build_shortlist` calls
`load_certified_rows` (reads parquet only; raises `FileNotFoundError` if a quarter's parquet is
missing) → `build_sponsor_table` (groups certified rows by `EMP_NORM`) → `verify.run_all(rows,
table)` which **raises and stops** on the first failed check → writes `sponsors_levelI.csv` (one
row/employer) + `sponsors_levelI_rows.csv` (row-level grounding for the portfolio review). `build_report`
reads only the CSV (+ optional `output/private/gap_read_filled.md`). The portfolio-review step touches
none of the pipeline — it's a manual step whose reviewed output is slotted into the report.

**Cold-start (empty clone → report).**
```bash
python3 -m venv .venv
.venv/bin/pip install -r requirements.txt
# Download DOL LCA quarters into data/raw/ named LCA_Disclosure_Data_FY2025_Q1.xlsx … Q4.xlsx
#   (dol.gov OFLC performance-data page; ~80–144 MB each)
.venv/bin/python scripts/convert_quarters.py    # raw xlsx -> data/processed/*.parquet (only missing; --force to redo)
.venv/bin/python scripts/build_shortlist.py     # verify (gate) + write output/sponsors_levelI.csv (+ _rows.csv)
.venv/bin/python scripts/build_report.py        # -> output/private/stay_here_report.html
# MANUAL portfolio review: fill the 4 blocks in prompts/gap_read.md, run in your own Claude/ChatGPT,
#   review every named-company claim, save to output/private/gap_read_filled.md, rerun build_report.py
```

**Prereqs.** Python 3.12 (per `__pycache__`) · `pandas`, `pyarrow`, `openpyxl` (`requests` is
listed but **unused** — see drift) · no external binary. Raw xlsx are large + gitignored.

**"Is it working?" signals.** convert → `converted FY2025Q4: N rows -> …` (or `exists, skip`).
build_shortlist → a `PROVENANCE` block with every quarter `[OK]`, a `VERIFICATION CELL` of `PASS —`
lines, then `RESULT: distinct sponsor employers : M` + a real Top-20 (Amazon/Deloitte/Activision/
Esri, not staffing-dominated) = **GO**; any `VerificationError` = **NO-GO**. build_report →
`wrote output/private/stay_here_report.html (M companies, R repeat sponsors)`.

**Human-in-the-loop.** (1) the **portfolio review** — run the prompt in your own LLM, review every
named-company claim before sharing the output; (2) the **OPT-now eyeball** — manually check each
pointed-to company for a live entry-level posting (LCA = "sponsors," not "hiring now"); (3)
**privacy** — never publish anything under `output/private/`.

**Failure / debug first-moves.** `No raw files in data/raw/` → download a quarter. `missing
required columns […]. Wrong file (PERM not LCA?)` → wrong/renamed file. `skip (unrecognized name)`
→ filename doesn't match `FILE_RE` (`convert_quarters.py:22`). `FileNotFoundError: Missing parquet`
→ run `convert_quarters` first. `VerificationError`: normalization-unit → `normalize_employer`
regression; non-empty → filter broke / wrong Level-I spelling for the vintage; **golden-killtest →
real parse drift OR you ran quarters without `IGAVEL`** (D3). Stale output → delete
`data/processed/*.parquet` and `convert --force`.

**Runtime state.** `data/processed/*.parquet` persists between runs (delete or `--force` to
invalidate). `output/*` is regenerated every run. `output/private/gap_read_filled.md` is
human-authored and persists; `build_report` slots it in if present, else shows a placeholder.

</details>

---

## 3 · Each core component — broken down (decisions nested inside)

<details>
<summary><b>A · The engine</b> — <code>engine/sponsors.py</code> · the deterministic Layer-1 core</summary>

**Role.** The whole "what the product *is*" on the data side: turn raw DOL filings into the
grounded sponsor shortlist. It's the reusable, role-parameterized function a future self-serve UI
would call — it returns DataFrames and imports nothing about HTML/PDF (that separability is half
of D4).

**How it works (first-principles, three stages).**
1. **Ingest** — `xlsx_to_parquet` streams a 100+ MB raw xlsx and writes a narrow 12-column
   parquet cache (D1). Runs once per quarter.
2. **Filter** — `load_certified_rows` reads the parquet(s) and keeps rows that are
   `CASE_STATUS == CERTIFIED` **∩** `SOC7 ∈ role's SOC set` **∩** `PW_WAGE_LEVEL ∈ Level-I
   spellings`, drops blank-employer rows, and derives `EMP_NORM` / `SOC7` / `WAGE_ANNUAL`. Output
   = one row per certified filing (this is the grounding the portfolio review consumes).
3. **Aggregate** — `build_sponsor_table` groups those rows by `EMP_NORM`, counts filings, counts
   distinct quarters, rolls up SOC titles / states / cities / wage min-median-max, and **sorts by
   `quarters_present` then `filing_count`** (D5). Output = one row per employer = the shortlist.
   Role generality lives in `ROLE_SOC` (`:32`) — adding "consultant" later is one dict entry (D4/#7).

**Key decisions inside A.**

**▸ D1 — xlsx→parquet: streaming openpyxl, by-name 12-col select · Open · full · maint·cost · `[code]`**
- **The call.** `engine/sponsors.py:134` `xlsx_to_parquet`: open the raw xlsx with
  `openpyxl(read_only=True)`, read the header, **resolve the 12 `REQUIRED_COLUMNS` by name** (not
  index), pull only those, coerce wages numeric / rest to string, write a narrow parquet;
  downstream reads parquet only.
- **Why (tied to role + lens).** `read_only=True` streams rows lazily (the workbook XML is never
  built into objects). **By-name** resolution survives that DOL's *column order is not stable
  across quarters* (`[doc]` B4 records a real index bug this caught: FY2025 Q1 has
  `WORKSITE_COUNTY` where Q4 has `WORKSITE_CITY`) — that's the **maintainability** win. Parquet
  then gives typed, fast repeat reads so the slow parse happens once — the **cost** win.
- **Option landscape (on the axes).**

  | option | peak memory | speed | deps | schema-drift | verdict |
  |--------|-------------|-------|------|--------------|---------|
  | `pandas.read_excel` | ✗ high (loads all) | slow | pandas | by-name ok | OOM risk on 144 MB |
  | **stream openpyxl (chosen)** | ~ok | slow-ish | boring/already-implied | by-name ✓ | rows still materialized (see below) |
  | `python-calamine` | low | **fast** (Rust) | newer dep | by-name ✓ | big speedup, v2 candidate |
  | `polars.read_excel` | low | fast | new dep | ✓ | also calamine-backed |
  | `duckdb` | low | fast | new dep | query | better for the v2 query path |
- **Why it wins here.** A handful of one-off quarterly parses → "low memory + boring already-needed
  deps + robust to DOL column churn" beats raw speed. `[likely-why]`
- **Would you make this call again?** Yes for v0. `[reconsider]` Two catches: (a) `:159`
  `rows = [tuple(r[i] for i in idx) for r in ws.iter_rows(...)]` materializes **every** row into a
  Python list before the DataFrame — it streams the parse but **not memory**; profile it on the
  144 MB quarter. (b) For the v2 automated refresh, swap to calamine/polars + duckdb.
- **Rebuild path.** Read one 100 MB quarter three ways (`read_excel`, stream-openpyxl, calamine),
  record peak RSS + wall time; then re-read only 12 cols via calamine and feel the speedup. You'll
  own *why* openpyxl is the safe-not-fast pick and exactly what you'd trade to switch.

**▸ D2 — Employer normalization: rule-based, conservative under-merge · Open · full · UX·maint · `[code]`**
- **The call.** `engine/sponsors.py:94` `normalize_employer`: uppercase → strip punctuation → drop
  trailing corporate suffixes (`LLC/INC/CORP/LTD/…`) → drop a leading `THE`; `None` for blank
  names (so they group and get excluded). `build_sponsor_table` groups on this `EMP_NORM`.
- **Why (tied to role + lens).** Collapses spelling/entity variants ("The Deloitte Consulting,
  LLP." + "Deloitte Consulting LLP" → `DELOITTE CONSULTING`) so one employer = one row and the
  filing count is real. Deliberately **conservative**: it won't merge genuinely different legal
  spellings — because a **false merge misleads the user's career bet (UX/correctness)**, which is
  worse than splitting a count slightly. Over-merge is the expensive error, so it under-merges on purpose.
- **Option landscape.** exact match (splinters variants) · **rule-based suffix strip (chosen)**
  (precise, explainable; misses `IBM`↔`International Business Machines`) · fuzzy/`rapidfuzz`
  (more recall, risks false merges) · record-linkage/`dedupe` (overkill at this scale) · canonical
  key (EIN/DUNS) · small curated alias map (cheap recall boost).
- **Why it wins here.** The shortlist is tens–low-hundreds of employers you can eyeball, and it
  underwrites a real career bet → **precision + explainability > recall**. `[doc]`
- **Would you make this call again?** Yes for v0 at this scale. `[reconsider]` Add a small curated
  alias map before reaching for fuzzy; revisit only if the list outgrows eyeball size or feeds an
  automated product.
- **Rebuild path.** Normalize 30 real `EMPLOYER_NAME` values by rule; run `rapidfuzz` at ratio 90
  then 80 and watch a false merge appear; add a 5-entry alias map and see recall rise without the
  false-merge risk. You'll own the precision/recall dial.

**▸ D5 — Full-year roll-up + repeat-sponsor ranking · OWNED · confirm · UX · `[doc]`**
`build_sponsor_table` sorts by `quarters_present` first (`:277`), then `filing_count`. The claim
"*actually* sponsors entry-wage designers" is far stronger for a company seen across multiple
quarters than a single spike — that's the **UX/quality** of the shortlist the user reads. Because the
engine is quarter-parameterized, full-year was a one-call extension, not new scope (single quarter
stays the documented kill-test). `decision_log` B2. Owned.

**▸ D6 — Level-I spelling tolerance + wage annualization · OWNED · confirm · maint · `[code]`**
`LEVEL_I_VALUES = {"I","LEVEL I","1"}` (`:66`) tolerates how `PW_WAGE_LEVEL` is spelled across DOL
vintages (the comment says `value_counts()` before trusting a new vintage; FY2025 uses bare `"I"`).
`_annualized_wage` multiplies `WAGE_RATE_OF_PAY_FROM` by a unit factor so wages compare across
HOUR/YEAR/etc. — explicitly a *secondary* display signal, not a filter. Small data-vintage
robustness calls; owned. **Watch:** an unknown wage unit silently yields `None` (acceptable, since
wage is secondary).

**Growth (what A's shape enables/constrains).** Role-parameterization + report-agnostic return
make new roles and a future query UI cheap (load-bearing, and it's the right load-bearing). The
constraint that will bite first is D1's in-memory materialization if this ever runs at automated
full-corpus scale.

</details>

<details>
<summary><b>B · The verification gate</b> — <code>engine/verify.py</code> · the raise-and-stop "cell"</summary>

**Role.** Make it impossible to *silently* ship a bogus shortlist someone bets a career on. A
failed check raises `VerificationError` and stops the run before any CSV is written.

**How it works.** `run_all` runs six checks in order: `verify_normalization_unit`
(data-independent), `verify_columns`, `verify_nonempty`, `verify_count_consistency`
(Σ filing_count == selected rows), `verify_collapse` (a multi-spelling employer collapses to one
row, else "grouping reduced rows"), `verify_golden_killtest` (top kill-test sponsor present). Five
are **quarter-independent invariants** (they hold for any quarter); one is a **golden anchor**.

**Key decision inside B.**

**▸ D3 — Verification = invariants + one golden anchor · REVISIT · full · maint · `[code]`**
- **Why (lens).** Invariants (units, schema, count-consistency, collapse) travel across quarters →
  **maintainable** across DOL's churn; the golden anchor catches "the parse silently drifted" that
  pure schema checks miss. `[doc]` (B4: an assert caught the real index bug mid-build.)
- **Option landscape.** no checks · ad-hoc prints · **invariant asserts + one golden anchor
  (chosen)** · `pytest` suite · schema validation (`pandera`) · property-based (`hypothesis`). The
  chosen mix is invariant-heavy (robust) with a single sharp drift detector.
- **The residual (why REVISIT).** `verify_golden_killtest` defaults to `IGAVEL`
  (`engine/verify.py:86`), an FY2025-**Q4** employer, and `build_shortlist` calls `run_all`
  against *whatever* `--quarters` you pass, with **no override** (`:65`). Run a quarters-subset
  without iGavel and it raises on **correct** data — the prior build's exact failure mode, now
  shrunk to 1 of 6 checks. **Fix:** gate the golden anchor to a committed **fixture quarter**
  (assert it only when that quarter is in scope); let arbitrary quarters pass on invariants +
  schema only. (This is First Change #1 below.)
- **Rebuild path.** Add a `pandera` parquet schema + a `hypothesis` idempotence test for
  `normalize_employer`; then make the golden check take a fixture quarter and feel the difference
  between "input changed" and "parse drifted."

**Growth.** Invariant-first design is CI-ready as-is; the golden anchor is the one piece not yet
safe to run on arbitrary input. Fixing D3 unlocks "run any quarter/subset in CI."

</details>

<details>
<summary><b>C · The runners</b> — <code>scripts/convert_quarters.py</code>, <code>scripts/build_shortlist.py</code> · thin callers (the "altitude")</summary>

**Role.** Orchestrate the engine without holding logic: discover + convert raw files, print full
provenance, run the gate, write artifacts. They're the reduced "notebook" — diffable and reviewable.

**How it works.** `convert_quarters` globs `data/raw/LCA_Disclosure_Data_*.xlsx`, matches
`FILE_RE` (`:22`), and converts each **missing** quarter to parquet (`--force` to redo).
`build_shortlist` prints a `PROVENANCE` block (role, SOCs, wage level, quarters, per-quarter parquet
status), calls the engine (`load_certified_rows` + `build_sponsor_table`), runs `verify.run_all`
as a gate, prints `RESULT` + Top-20, and writes `sponsors_levelI.csv` + `sponsors_levelI_rows.csv`.

**Key decision inside C.**

**▸ D4 — Engine/altitude split · OWNED · confirm · maint · `[doc]`**
`engine/` holds all logic and imports nothing about reports; `scripts/*` are thin callers. This is
`decision_log` #6 (the Level-I parse *is* the engine a future self-serve UI calls) and it's
faithfully built — `build_sponsor_table` is role-parameterized and report-agnostic. Owned. **Nit:**
the four default quarters live in `build_shortlist.py:30` while paths/columns live in the engine; if
more constants spread across scripts, lift them to one config.

**Growth.** The split is exactly what makes a v2 self-serve UI a re-use of A rather than a rewrite.
Keep new logic out of the scripts or the altitude line blurs.

</details>

<details>
<summary><b>D · The report</b> — <code>scripts/build_report.py</code> · the private one-pager</summary>

**Role.** Render the shortlist + caveats + (when present) the user's 3 named-company projects into one
shareable, self-contained HTML for early UX feedback. Presentation only — reads the CSV, writes HTML.

**How it works.** Reads `output/sponsors_levelI.csv`, renders an embedded-CSS HTML page (stats,
UX/UI badges for SOC 15-1255, shortlist table, caveats, provenance footer). The portfolio-review section is
slotted from `output/private/gap_read_filled.md` via a minimal hand-rolled `md_to_basic_html`; if
absent, a clearly-marked placeholder shows. Output path is `output/private/` (gitignored).

**Key decisions inside D.**

**▸ D7 — Self-contained HTML (no JS/pandoc) · OWNED · confirm · UX·cost · `[doc]`**
One HTML file, embedded CSS, no JS, no external assets, no pandoc. `decision_log` B7: pandoc isn't
installed; a single self-contained file opens anywhere and is trivially shareable — **UX** (opens
anywhere) at near-zero **cost** (no new deps). Owned. **Watch:** `md_to_basic_html` handles only
headings/bullets/bold — fine for the controlled portfolio-review input, not general markdown.

**▸ D8 — Privacy split · OWNED · confirm · cost-risk·UX · `[doc]`**
The user's portfolio-derived report → `output/private/` (gitignored); the engine, method, decision log,
and the **public-record** LCA shortlist CSV are shareable. `decision_log` B6. The right boundary
(LCA employer names are public record; a user's job-search/portfolio data is not). Owned. **Watch:** the
boundary is `.gitignore` + a "Private" footer — *convention, not enforcement*; if this ever goes
multi-user it must be enforced in code.

**Growth.** The report is the throwaway skin; A/B are the durable core. Don't let report needs pull
logic back down into the engine.

</details>

<details>
<summary><b>E · The portfolio review</b> — <code>prompts/gap_read.md</code> · the human-reviewed advice half</summary>

**Role.** The "advice" half of the differentiator: turn the grounded shortlist into 3
named-company portfolio projects. It is a **prompt run outside the codebase**, in your own
Claude/ChatGPT, with you as the reviewer before the output is shared.

**How it works.** You fill four blocks (`[[PORTFOLIO]]`, `[[SHORTLIST]]` rows, `[[LIVE_POSTINGS]]`
you gather by hand, `[[TARGET_ROLE]]`), run it, review, and save the approved output to
`output/private/gap_read_filled.md`, which `build_report` slots in. It also carries the **OPT-now
eyeball** (manual "are they hiring now?" per company) that stands in for the cut Layer A.

**Key decision inside E.**

**▸ D9 — AI confined to a human-reviewed portfolio review; data step deterministic · OWNED · confirm · UX-trust · `[doc]`**
The engine is **NO-LLM** and deterministic; the only AI is this portfolio review, human-reviewed.
`decision_log` #5: AI on the deterministic parse would inject ungrounded error into the moat
(grounding) — so keeping it out is what protects the **trust/correctness (UX)** the whole product
sells. This is the product's trust spine; owned. **Watch:** the guarantee is *convention* (a prompt
file + "you review"), not enforced — if a future version automates the portfolio review, the human-review
boundary disappears and the grounding claim weakens.

**Growth.** Productizing means making this boundary a *feature* (visible "reviewed by a human"),
not automating it away. That's the axis to defend if this ever scales.

</details>

---

<details>
<summary><b>4 · Drift — built vs <code>docs/decision_log.md</code></b> (this build is unusually clean)</summary>

**Faithful to the ledger:** advisory diagnostic not a directory (#1) ✓ · `PW_WAGE_LEVEL=I` signal
(#2) ✓ · raw LCA source (#3) ✓ · designer ICP via `ROLE_SOC` (#4,#7) ✓ · AI judgment-only (#5) ✓ ·
engine/altitude split (#6) ✓ · **Layer A (OPT-now postings pipeline) cut to v2** (#8) ✓ · full-year
roll-up (B2) ✓ · FY2025 + bare-`"I"` (B3) ✓ · by-name columns (B4) ✓ · module+script not notebook
(B5) ✓ · privacy split (B6) ✓ · self-contained HTML (B7) ✓.

**Headline:** there is **no scraper / no `ingest_portfolio.py`** — the portfolio input is manual via
`prompts/gap_read.md`, exactly as the ledger's Layer-A cut + Layer-3 "portfolio as text/links" intended.
The build *matches its design.*

**Minor drift / cruft to own:**
- **`requests>=2.31` in `requirements.txt` but never imported** — no download script exists
  (downloads are manual per the README). Either wire a fetch helper or drop the dep. Smells like an
  unfinished "auto-download quarters" intent. (First Change #2.)
- **No standalone kill-test entrypoint.** The ledger's B1 kill-test is folded into
  `build_shortlist`'s provenance + `verify_golden_killtest` rather than a separate script.
  Defensible (B1 is framed as a one-time manual run), but there's no single GO/NO-GO command — it's
  implicit in reading `build_shortlist` output.

**Open tension (carried, not a bug):** golden anchor (`IGAVEL`, FY2025 Q4) vs "run any
quarter/subset" — the ledger doesn't address how golden checks behave on arbitrary quarters (D3).

</details>

<details>
<summary><b>5 · Gaps ranked</b> (decision + runtime)</summary>

1. **D1 xlsx-IO landscape + "streaming" memory reality** `[decision + runtime]` — most AI-defaulted
   call and the engine's foundation; `:159` materializes all rows. Biggest re-derivation gap;
   matters most for the v2 automation goal.
2. **D3 golden-anchor residual quarter-coupling** `[runtime + decision]` — small, well-understood;
   gate the anchor to a fixture quarter. The shrunken descendant of the prior build's #1 bug.
3. **D2 entity-resolution landscape** `[decision]` — the conservative under-merge is defensible but
   its alternatives (fuzzy / record-linkage / EIN / alias-map) are unplaced. One toy build closes it.
4. **Full-year memory/scaling** `[runtime]` — convert + load + `concat` 4 quarters (one 144 MB raw);
   peak-memory/timing profile is uncharacterized. Matters if it ever runs in CI or on a modest laptop.
5. **`requests` unused dep / no download automation** `[hygiene / drift]` — remove it or finish the
   intent; today it's a dependency that does nothing.

</details>

---

## 6 · Own it — the active pass (turn reading-ownership into the real thing)

<details open>
<summary><b>Code to review</b> — the ~6 spots where your own eyes are worth it (skip the rest; trust it from this doc)</summary>

| # | Where | Read it because | Look for |
|---|-------|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | `engine/sponsors.py:134–172` `xlsx_to_parquet` | the most AI-defaulted call + the foundation | the by-name resolution (`:150–158`) **and** `:159` materializing every row — confirm "streaming" is parse-only, not memory |
| 2 | `engine/sponsors.py:178–222` `load_certified_rows` | this *is* the filter that defines the product | the `Certified ∩ SOC ∩ Level-I` chain + the `wage_level=="I"` expansion to `LEVEL_I_VALUES` (`:194`) + the `None`-employer drop (`:220`) |
| 3 | `engine/sponsors.py:262–291` `build_sponsor_table` | the ranking *is* the product claim | groupby `EMP_NORM` + sort by `quarters_present` first (`:277`) — the "actually sponsors" signal |
| 4 | `engine/sponsors.py:94–112` `normalize_employer` | the under-merge that protects the user's career bet | the suffix strip + leading-`THE` drop; imagine where it false-merges vs misses |
| 5 | `engine/verify.py:86–101` `verify_golden_killtest` + `run_all` | the REVISIT edge | that `IGAVEL` is hardcoded and `run_all` is called unconditionally in `build_shortlist.py:65` |
| 6 | `docs/decision_log.md` (whole) | it's the design source-of-truth + drift anchor | read once end-to-end; it's short and it's your interview script |

**Trust from this doc, don't spend eyes on:** the HTML/CSS f-string in `build_report.py` (D7), the
provenance-printing plumbing in `build_shortlist.py`, the package re-exports in `engine/__init__.py`.

</details>

<details open>
<summary><b>First changes to make it yours</b> — 3 small real edits (doing-ownership)</summary>

1. **Gate the golden anchor to a fixture quarter (closes D3, the #1 open code task).** Make
   `verify_golden_killtest` assert `IGAVEL` **only when its fixture quarter (FY2025Q4) is in
   scope**; otherwise pass on invariants. Touches `engine/verify.py` + the `run_all` call site in
   `build_shortlist.py`. **Buys:** you own the verification-vs-input-brittleness axis by fixing the
   exact residual — and the pipeline becomes safe to run on any quarter/subset (CI-ready).
2. **Resolve the `requests` drift.** Remove `requests>=2.31` from `requirements.txt` (confirm
   nothing imports it — it doesn't) *or* write the tiny download helper it implies. **Buys:** you
   own the dependency surface and clear a real drift flag; forces you to prove the import graph.
3. **Add one real test to the engine.** A `hypothesis` idempotence test for `normalize_employer`
   (`normalize(normalize(x)) == normalize(x)`) or a small fixture-based test that
   `build_sponsor_table` ranks a 2-quarter employer above a 1-quarter one. **Buys:** converts the
   "verification cell" *convention* into an actual test; doing-ownership of the load-bearing engine.

</details>

<details>
<summary><b>Drills</b> — prove it without the code in front of you</summary>

**Onboarding check.** From this doc alone: say what Stay Here is and its current state, draw the
5-component map, and justify the top 3 decisions (D1, D3, D2) on maintainability/UX/cost — no source.

**Runtime cold-trace.** Trace one `EMPLOYER_NAME` cell: raw xlsx row → `xlsx_to_parquet` →
`load_certified_rows` (filter) → `normalize_employer` → `groupby("EMP_NORM")` → a row in
`sponsors_levelI.csv`, naming each function + file. Then: *"`build_shortlist` just raised
`VerificationError` on `verify_golden_killtest` — real parse drift or wrong quarters? How do I tell
in 30 seconds?"* (Answer: check whether FY2025Q4/`IGAVEL` is in the `--quarters` you ran.)

**Decision self-interview (cold).** Three ways to read a 100 MB xlsx and when each wins · why
parquet over CSV for the interchange · merge `Amazon.com Services LLC` + `AMAZON.COM SERVICES, INC.`
without merging Apple Inc / Apple Bank · which `verify` checks survive a new quarter and which one
doesn't, and why · why `quarters_present` beats `filing_count` as the sort key · why the engine is
NO-LLM and what breaks if AI touches the parse · where the public/private boundary lives and how it
must change for multi-user.

</details>

---

*Success test for this doc: a fresh engineer reads it and can say what Stay Here is, draw the
component map, and justify D1/D2/D3 on maintainability/UX/cost — without opening the code. If a
section fails that bar, fix the skill and regenerate, don't hand-patch this file.*
